coredata.dc:title: Aqueous synthesis of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots and their application as surfactants for preparing of fluorescent polymer microspheres
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: He, Jingbo
Li, Zhiyang
Zhao, Rujian
Lu, Yao
Shi, Lanqi
Liu, Jiyang
Dong, Xiaoping
Xi, Fengna
coredata.dc:description: Amphiphilic carbon-based nanoparticles have recently attracted much attention in stabilizing Pickering emulsions owing to their low cytotoxicity, cost-effectiveness and biocompatibility. In this work, a facile and aqueous synthesis method was developed for the preparation of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots by modifying hydrophilic GQDs (GQDs-COOH) with hydrophobic hexylamine. Distinctive from pristine GQDs-COOH, amphiphilic GQDs show interesting surface-active properties and can stabilize water-in-cyclohexane Pickering emulsions. As efficient particle surfactants, amphiphilic GQDs could be applied in a mini-emulsion polymerization system to synthesize submicron-sized polystyrene particles. Particularly, the as-prepared PS particles were highly luminescent as produced by the highly fluorescent amphiphilic GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic graphene quantum dots
Aqueous synthesis
Mini-emulsion polymerization
Particle surfactant
Luminescent polystyrene microspheres

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amphiphilic GQDs in aqueous medium
Preparation of Pickering emulsions stabilized by amphiphilic GQDs
Fabrication of polystyrene microspheres with amphiphilic GQDs as particle surfactants
Preparation of Pickering emulsions stabilized by amphiphilic GQDs
Preparation of luminescent polymer microsphere using amphiphilic GQDs as particle surfactant

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305127 ), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LY15B050006 , LY17B050007 ), the Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University ( 13062173-Y ) and 521 talent project of ZSTU .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of amphiphilic GQDs and their application as particle surfactants for the preparation of fluorescent polymer microspheres by mini-emulsion polymerization.

(A) Photograph of Pickering emulsions stabilized by amphiphilic GQDs prepared with different amounts of hexylamine. The concentrations of hexylamine in the reaction medium for sample 1–5 are 5.4, 6.9, 8.4, 10.0, 11.5 mg/mL, respectively. (B–F) present optical microscope photographs of water-in-cyclohexane drops of Pickering emulsion in sample 1–5 of A (The scale bar is 100 μm).

(A) TEM image of amphiphilic GQDs. Inset presents HRTEM image and the size distribution of amphiphilic GQDs. (B) AFM image of amphiphilic GQDs. Inset is the corresponding height profiles along the indicated line.
XPS survey spectrum (A) and high resolution C1 s (B), N1 s (C), and O1 s (D) spectra of the amphiphilic GQDs.

(A)FTIR spectra of GQDs-COOH and amphiphilic GQDs. (B) fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of amphiphilic GQDs. The EM spectra were obtained with different excitation wavelength (from bottom to top were 420, 425, 430, 435, 440, 445, 450, 455, 460 nm, respectively). Inset presents the images of aqueous solution of amphiphilic GQDs under natural light (left) and UV light (right).

SEM images of PS microsphere prepared with (A) 0.64 mg/mL, (B) 0.96 mg/mL, (C) 1.28 mg/mL amphiphilic GQDs being particle surfactant. (D–F) are energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping images of C (D), O (E) and N (F) elements.

(A)Photographic images of solutions after emulsion polymerization with the addition of GQDs-COOH (left) and amphiphilic GQDs (right). (B) Bright-field and (C) fluorescence microscope images of PS microsphere prepared with amphiphilic GQDs as particle surfactant.

head.ce:title: Aqueous synthesis of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots and their application as surfactants for preparing of fluorescent polymer microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 5 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amphiphilic carbon-based nanoparticles have recently attracted much attention in stabilizing Pickering emulsions owing to their low cytotoxicity, cost-effectiveness and biocompatibility. In this work, a facile and aqueous synthesis method was developed for the preparation of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots by modifying hydrophilic GQDs (GQDs-COOH) with hydrophobic hexylamine. Distinctive from pristine GQDs-COOH, amphiphilic GQDs show interesting surface-active properties and can stabilize water-in-cyclohexane Pickering emulsions. As efficient particle surfactants, amphiphilic GQDs could be applied in a mini-emulsion polymerization system to synthesize submicron-sized polystyrene particles. Particularly, the as-prepared PS particles were highly luminescent as produced by the highly fluorescent amphiphilic GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Amphiphiles, that possess both hydrophilic (water-loving, polar) and lipophilic (fat-loving, non-polar) properties, form the basis for a number of research areas in chemistry, biochemistry and materials [1–3]. Nowadays, amphiphilic nanoparticles act as novel particle surfactants and have attracted great interests in stabilizing Pickering emulsions for application in food, cosmetics and pharmaceutical fields [4–8]. In contrast to conventional molecule-type surfactant, that usually derives from petrochemical industry and increases the environmental impact, amphiphilic nanoparticles surfactants exhibit mechanically robust monolayer at the oil-water interface and generate highly stable emulsions. Moreover, interesting photonic, magnetic, electrical, or catalytic properties could be possibly introduced [9–11]. Comparing to other amphiphilic nanoparticles including silica, titania nanoparticles, zirconium phosphate, protein microgel, clay, and polymer particles [12–17], carbon-based nanoparticles have recently attracted much attention owing to their low cytotoxicity, cost-effectiveness and biocompatibility [18,19]. For example, graphene oxide (GO) has been firstly used as a particle surfactant because of the amphiphilicity resulting from both the hydrophobic graphene plane and hydrophilic oxygen groups on the edge [20,21]. However, the stability of GO-based submicron-sized emulsions is unsatisfactory owing to the curvature effect caused by the large size. In addition, practical application might be limited in bioimaging and optical fields without luminescent properties. Therefore, realizing new amphiphilic carbon nanoparticles to form stable emulsion and exploring their functional applications is of great significance.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are atomically thin and nanometer-wide planar structures of graphene. Compared to the conventional fluorescent materials, GQDs possess unique merits including tunable photoluminescence (PL), exceptional physicochemical properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent photostability [22–27]. Recent advances in the synthesis of GQDs allow them to be formed from fine carbon structures (e.g. graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, etc) by top-down methods, or from small chemical precursors (e.g. citric acid, glucose, etc) by bottom-up approaches [22,28]. However, almost all GQDs can only be used either in aqueous phase or oil phase. Recently, it has been proven that amphiphilic GQDs could be synthesized by chemical-grafting on hydrophilic or hydrophobic GQDs. As compared to their oil soluble or water soluble counterparts, amphiphilic GQDs have broader applications due to the retained luminescence properties and new surface properties. For instance, Yang et al. prepared amphiphilic GQDs by the thermal reduction of oxygen-containing groups at GO. The as-prepared GQDs can be applied as surfactants to produce Pickering emulsions, prepare polymer particles and generate block copolymer particles [29]. They also synthesized amphiphilic GQDs by grafting different alkyl ligands (hexylamine and oleylamine) to the hydrophilic GQDs to generate the surfactant behavior. The grafting process was performed in tetrahydrofuran (THF) and the product was purified by diethyl ether [30]. Xuan et al. prepared dodecylamine-functionalized GQDs via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and dodecylamine in an ammonium hydroxide solution [31]. Cho et al. grafted hydrophobic hexylamine onto hydrophilic GQDs by N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) coupling and epoxide ring opening in THF plus 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP) medium [32]. Zeng et al. prepared C18-GQDs using carbonization between citric acid and octadecylamine [33]. Li et al. prepared histidine-functionalized GQDs (His-GQDs) via a thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and histidine. After Zn2+ reacted with the His-GQD to produce Zn-His-GQD framework nanoparticles by coordination bonds, the product exhibit a better amphiphilicity [34]. As the present methods for the synthesis of amphiphilic GQDs usually require tedious steps or organic solvent, simple and aqueous synthesis is highly desirable.

In this work, a facile and aqueous synthesis method is developed for the preparation of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots and their application for the preparation of luminescent polymer is presented. As shown in Fig. 1 , grafting of hydrophobic hexylamine on hydrophilic GQDs with carboxyl groups (GQDs-COOH) is easily achieved with catalyzed formation of amide bonds in aqueous medium. The as-prepared amphiphilic GQDs can stabilize water-in-oil reverse Pickering emulsions. Owning to the interesting surface-active properties, amphiphilic GQDs are applied in a mini-emulsion polymerization system to synthesize submicron-sized polystyrene (PS) particles with highly luminescent.

The CX-72 carbon black was purchased from Cabot Corporation (USA). Concentrated nitric acid (AR) was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng chemical reagent CO., Ltd (China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDCl, 98%), hexylamine, styrene (AR), K2S2O8 (AR) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH = 5.6) was prepared by Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Milli-Q, Millipore).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd, Japan). The operating voltage was 200 kV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were obtained with tapping mode by a Bruker Multimode 8 (Bruker. Inc, USA). The surface wettability of GQDs film was evaluated by measuring the water contact angle through a contact angle goniometer (DCA-322, Themo Cahn, USA). Surface tension was measured using surface tension meter (K100Mk2, Metrohm, Switzerland). Elemental analysis was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a PHI5300 electron spectrometer using 250 W, 14 kV, Mg Ka radiation (PE Ltd, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were taken using infrared spectrometer (NICOLET iS10, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) spectra and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping images were taken using a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (vltra55, Carl Zeiss SMT Pte Ltd, Germany). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer (LS45, PerkinElmer, UK). The fluorescence of polymer spheres was characterized by a fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amphiphilic GQDs in aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para: Amphiphilic GQDs was prepared by grafting of hydrophobic hexylamine on hydrophilic GQDs with carboxyl groups (GQDs-COOH) via EDC/NHS catalyzed formation of amide bonds in aqueous medium. Firstly, GQDs-COOH was prepared by refluxing CX-72 carbon black powders with nitric acid (6 M) for 24 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. After evaporating the solvent of the obtained supernatant by heating, GQDs-COOH were obtained as reddish-brown powder. Secondly, amphiphilic GQDs were synthesized by covalent connection of carboxyl group on GQDs-COOH and amino group of hexylamine. Briefly, GQDs-COOH (6 mg/mL), EDCl (10 mg/mL) and NHS (10 mg/mL) were dissolved in PBS (20 mL, pH = 5.6) before hexylamine was added. The above solution was stirred for 4 h at 60 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was then dialyzed with dialysis membrane (500 MW) for 24 h to remove unreacted small molecules. To optimize the preparation process, different weight of hexylamine (5.4, 6.9, 8.4, 10.0, 11.5 mg/mL) was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pickering emulsions stabilized by amphiphilic GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solution of amphiphilic GQDs at 3.5 mg/mL was prepared. After mixed with cyclohexane (v:v 1:1), the obtained mixture was shook vigorously to form pickering emulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of polystyrene microspheres with amphiphilic GQDs as particle surfactants
ce:section.ce:para: Polystyrene (PS) microspheres were prepared in a 50 mL flask. Briefly, amphiphilic GQDs (1.6 mg/mL) and styrene (0.3 mg/mL) were dispersed in deionized water and stirred vigorously for 5 min. Then K2S2O8 (6.0 mg/mL) was added to the flask and sonicated for 20 min in an ice water bath. After that, the mixture is degassed three times with nitrogen before carrying out the polymerization reaction. The mixture was stirred at 75 °C for 8 h. Then the PS colloidal solution was purified by centrifugation and the precipitated product was washed several times with ultrapure water to obtain PS spheres solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pickering emulsions stabilized by amphiphilic GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In present work, amphiphilic GQDs was prepared by grafting of hydrophobic hexylamine on hydrophilic GQDs with carboxyl groups (GQDs-COOH) via EDC/NHS catalyzed formation of amide bonds in aqueous medium. As GQDs-COOH is hydrophilic, the introduction of hydrophobic hexylamine is vital. Thus the concentration of hexylamine in the reaction process was optimized and the obtained products were applied to stabilize Pickering emulsion. Water and cyclohexane system was chosen as the model of immiscible biphasic system. When the addition amount of hexylamine is small, as shown in Fig. 2 A, most as-prepared GQDs stayed in the water phase and only a small amount of emulsification can be observed at the interface of water and cyclohexane. In contrast, the GQDs prepared at high amount of hexylamine can form clean and stable reverse-phase emulsion (water-in-cyclohexane emulsion) in hexylamine phase. The formed Pickering emulsion exhibit mirror reflection. The emulsion drops were also characterized by optical microscope for further intuitionistic investigation. As demonstrated in Fig. 2B-F, the emulsion is composed by spherical droplets. The number of droplets and the uniformity of droplet size increased with the increase of the concentration of reactant hexylamine. Thus, the concentration of hexylamine was fixed at 10 mg/mL for further investigation. When the water-in-cyclohexane Pickering emulsion was stored for different time, no significant changes were observed at the optical microscope photos, indicating high stability of the Pickering emulsion (Fig. S1). To evaluate the surface property of amphiphilic GQDs, contact angle of water on amphiphilic GQDs film was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, a contact angle of 33.9° is observed. The value is higher than that of hydrophilic GQDs-COOH (14.8°), indicating efficient tuning of hydrophilic-hydrophobic balance of GQDs by modification with hexylamine (Fig. S2). Fig. S3 reveals the surface tension of aqueous solution containing amphiphilic GQDs or hydrophilic GQDs-COOH. As seen, GQDs-COOH solution has similar surface tension with pure water. On the contrary, the surface tension of aqueous solution containing amphiphilic GQDs is low and remarkably decreases when the concentration of amphiphilic GQDs increases (Fig. S3). Thus, the amphiphilic GQDs show potential as surfactants by reducing surface tension of aqueous solution.

As demonstrated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), amphiphilic GQDs exhibited size range between 1.8–5.4 nm with average size of 3.6 nm (Fig. 3 A). High-resolution TEM image (HRTEM) reveals the good crystallinity of amphiphilic GQDs with lattice spacing of 0.23 nm (Fig. 3A inset), which is corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [35]. Atomic force microscopy shows that the typical thickness of amphiphilic GQDs is ∼1.5 nm (Fig. 3B). Since the chain length of the extended hexylamine molecule was about 1 nm at the equilibrium geometry, the as-prepared amphiphilic GQDs are mostly single-layered.

The composition of amphiphilic GQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4 A, three distinct peaks of C1 s, O1 s, N1 s are observed on the XPS survey spectrum of amphiphilic GQDs. The atomic concentrations are 66.97%, 29.45%, 3.58%, respectively. In the high resolution spectrum of C1 s (Fig. 4B), the binding energy peak at 285.0 eV represents the graphitic structure (CCC). The peaks at 286.6 eV, 288.6 eV and 289.5 eV could be respectively assigned to CN/CO, OCN and OCO [36,37]. The presence of OCN indicates covalent linking of hexylamine with GQDs-COOH via formation of amide bond. Two peaks for N1 s (Fig. 4C) have been resolved and assigned to CNC and amide (OCN) groups at 400.2 eV, 402.1 eV, respectively, further suggesting the formation of amine [38,39]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of the amphiphilic GQDs (Fig. 4D) exhibits two peaks at 531.7 eV, and 533.3 eV, suggesting the presence of the CO and CO [40]. Fig. 5 A gives the FTIR spectrum of both GQDs-COOH and amphiphilic GQDs. After introduction of hexylamine, the characteristic peaks of the amide bond and the alkyl chain can be observed. The peaks located at 2960 cm−1 and 2930 cm-1 represent the stretching vibration peaks of C–H bond. Compared with GQDs-COOH, the peaks of OCO at 1712 cm-1 reduced for amphiphilic GQDs. In contrast, new special peaks at 1640 cm−1, 1568 cm-1 and 1079 cm−1 appear corresponding to stretching vibration of CO, bending vibration of CNH and vibration of CN, proving the covalent linkage between hexylamine and GQDs-COOH through amine bonds [39,41].

As amphiphilic GQDs hold great promises as efficient particle surfactants for developing new emulsion-based luminescent materials, their fluorescent properties are investigated. Fig. 5B presents the excitation and emission spectra of amphiphilic GQDs. As seen, excitation of 460 nm results in the maximum emission at 512 nm with green emission. The emission of amphiphilic GQDs is unchanged with the change of excitation wavelength, indicating excitation-independent emission because of uniform structure. The excitation and emission spectra of the raw GQDs-COOH were compared as control. It was found that 10 nm blue shift exists for amphiphilic GQDs due to the covalent linkage of hexylamine that result in the reduce of carboxyl groups and the surface passivation by long chain (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of luminescent polymer microsphere using amphiphilic GQDs as particle surfactant
ce:section.ce:para: As amphiphilic GQDs exhibit interesting surface-active properties and can efficiently stabilize Pickering emulsion, their application as particle surfactants in miniemulsion polymerization of styrene is allowed. Styrene, K2S2O8 and amphiphilic GQDs aqueous solution were mixed to form stable Pickering emulsions. Similar with the reversed-phase Pickering emulsions in water-cyclohexane system, water-in-styrene emulsion formed in the mixture. It was supposed that amphiphilic GQDs adsorbed on interface of water-in-styrene drops, reduced surface energy and formed stable emulsion drops. When the temperature rises, the water-soluble initiator K2S2O8 produces free radicals, which triggers the emulsion polymerization of styrene and leads to the formation of polystyrene spheres (Fig. 1). The amount of added amphiphilic GQDs in the polymerization process was optimized. As shown in Fig. 6 sub-micronsized and spherical PS colloidal particles with smooth surfaces are observed, which is similar with the particles stabilized by conventional organic surfactants. Obviously, the stabilizing effect of amphiphilic GQDs can effectively prevent droplet dissolution and avoid the formation of a rigid interfacial layer. When the amount of added amphiphilic GQD in the polymerization system increased form 0.64 mg/mL to 1.28 mg/mL (Fig. 6 A–C), the homogeneity of PS microspheres increased. Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping images reveal that amphiphilic GQDs are composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements with uniform distribution (Fig. 6D-F). Among these elements, C element comes from GQDs and polystyrene structure. Oxygen and nitrogen elements could be attributed to the existence of amphiphilic GQDs on the surface of PS spheres. When the amount of amphiphilic GQDs further increased in the preparation of PS microspheres (Fig. S5), some little PS spheres were produced due to the excess surfactants. As the nucleation of the styrene oligomer is stabilized by the amphiphilic GQDs on its surface, the number of the nuclei of the oligomers increases and the size of the PS microspheres becomes smaller when the amount of GQDs increases. The conversions of the mini-emulsion polymerizations were greater than 90%, substantiating the effectiveness of the surfactant behaviors of amphiphilic GQDs. In contrast, PS colloidal particles were not produced when the raw GQDs-COOH were employed, proving the effective role of amphiphilic GQDs as surfactants in microemulsion polymerization (Fig. 7 A). As the amphiphilic GQDs surfactant is irreversibly adsorbed on the interface of the microemulsion droplets, GQDs will be combined into the PS microspheres with the polymerization. Fig. 7B and C represent the bright-field and fluorescence microscope images of the as-prepared PS microspheres. Bright green fluorescent presents even the PS microspheres are thoroughly washed for ten times to completely remove the non-specific adsorbed GQDs. That verifies the successful embedding of amphiphilic GQDs in PS microspheres. Therefore, the tunable luminescence emission of the amphiphilic GQDs could be synergistically combined with polymers and the composite materials might promise a large variety of applications in bioimaging, drug release and environmental researches, etc.

Amphiphilic graphene quantum dots were readily synthesized using catalyzed formation of amine bond in aqueous medium. Taking the advantages of their interesting surface-active propensities as well as the ability to effectively form Pickering emulsion, amphiphilic graphene quantum dots are employed as particle surfactants to form polystyrene spheres. The luminescence emission of the amphiphilic GQDs could be synergistically combined with polystyrene, demonstrating the potential applications in a large variety of practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305127), the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY15B050006, LY17B050007), the Science Foundation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (13062173-Y) and 521 talent project of ZSTU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2018.11.064.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
